\begin{document}

\title{Scattering, Scintillation, and Energetics of Fast Radio Bursts Codetected by CHIME/FRB and DSA-110}

% Author / affiliation block lives in auth.tex for reuse and clean diffs.
\input{auth.tex}

\begin{abstract}
% TODO(abstract): bracketed slots below are placeholders for withheld results;
% fill each only after the producing analysis clears re-validation, then delete
% this comment.
We present twelve fast radio bursts co-detected by CHIME/FRB
($0.4$--$0.8$\,GHz) and DSA-110 ($\sim$1.4\,GHz) between 2022 February and
2024 February. All twelve pass timing, dispersion, and positional association
tests, with chance-coincidence probabilities $P_{\rm cc}<10^{-8}$. The
$\sim$1\,GHz frequency baseline between the two facilities breaks the
degeneracy between scattering time and its frequency scaling that limits any
single-band measurement. Because the pulse-broadening function shape and the
scaling index $\alpha$ both descend from the same turbulence spectrum, we
co-model them through the spectral index $\beta$ of the electron-density
fluctuation spectrum, rather than fitting a fixed pulse-broadening shape with
$\alpha$ free---a combination that is physically inconsistent and biases the
inferred scaling.
[SLOT: joint two-band scattering results---validated $\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}$,
$\beta$, and multiplicity statements.]
A foreground census of all twelve sightlines, drawing on DESI Legacy, GLADE+,
SDSS, and cluster catalogs, finds a single sightline (FRB~20230307A) that
passes within $R_{500}$ of a foreground cluster ($b/R_{500}=0.83$), for which
a two-phase modified-NFW model predicts an intracluster dispersion column of
order $160\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$ (uncertain at the factor-of-two level,
$\approx100$--$560\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$ across the profile choice and
cluster-parameter systematics); the confirmed foreground galaxies on the
remaining sightlines sit in the outer circumgalactic medium and contribute
individually small columns.
[SLOT: dispersion-budget decomposition and host residuals.]
[SLOT: scintillation constraints and screen attribution.]
[SLOT: band-restricted burst energies.]
[SLOT: one-sentence physical conclusion tying scattering attribution to the
foreground census.]
\end{abstract}

[tool_result for=toolu_01AYQkoNDVR4e8C4LjpFrvw9] {"filename":"intro.tex","content_type":"text/x-latex","size_bytes":2715,"content":"\section{Introduction}
\label{sec:intro}

Fast radio bursts \citep[FRBs;][]{Lorimer2007, Thornton2013} provide compact,
bright backlights through ionized gas on Galactic, circumgalactic,
intergalactic, and host-galaxy scales. Their dispersion measures trace the
integrated electron column---anchoring the cosmological Macquart relation
\citep{Macquart2020}---while pulse broadening and diffractive scintillation
respond to turbulent structure and screen geometry
\citep[e.g.,][]{CordesChatterjee2019}. Individual sightlines have already
demonstrated the reach of these propagation diagnostics: scattering and
scintillation together can localize a screen to circumburst plasma
\citep{Masui2015}, limit the turbulent column of an intervening galaxy halo
\citep{Prochaska2019, Ocker2021}, or implicate the host interstellar medium as
the dominant scattering site \citep{Ocker2022}. At the population level, the
first CHIME/FRB catalog established that scattering times at 600\,MHz span
several orders of magnitude and are in tension with Galactic-only or IGM-only
origins \citep{CHIME2021Catalog, Chawla2022}. Yet jointly interpreting these
observables for any one burst remains degenerate: a given dispersion measure
can be distributed among multiple media, and a given scattering time can be
produced by different screen locations and turbulence spectra.

Co-detections by CHIME/FRB and DSA-110 help break these degeneracies by
measuring the same burst over a wide frequency baseline. CHIME/FRB samples
low frequencies at 400--800~MHz, where dispersion delays and scattering are
larger, while DSA-110 observes near 1.4~GHz, where the same event is less
broadened. A single-band measurement constrains only the scattering time at
that band's frequency; the $\sim$1\,GHz lever arm of a co-detection constrains
the frequency scaling of arrival-time structure and pulse broadening directly,
which no single dataset provides alone. It also introduces a critical
association step: the two detections must be demonstrated to be the same
astrophysical burst before their dispersion and scattering information can be
combined.

This paper is organized around that joint interpretation. We first define the
candidate co-detection sample and the data products used for each telescope. We
then describe the association test, the dispersion-budget model, the foreground
census used to identify potential intervening screens, the two-band scattering
model, the scintillation analysis, and the band-limited energy calculation.
The association tests, the foreground census, and the dispersion budget are
reported in full; the scattering, scintillation, turbulence, and energetics
results are developed in their respective sections.
"}

\label{sec:methods}

\input{sections/toa.tex}
\input{sections/budget.tex}

\subsection{Band-restricted burst energies}
\label{sec:methods-energies}

For each sightline with a spectroscopic host redshift and a trusted per-band
amplitude fit, we estimate the isotropic-equivalent burst energy directly
from the joint CHIME--DSA fit, without extrapolating either band's spectrum
beyond where it is constrained.
The fit returns a per-band spectral amplitude and index,
$F_X(\nu) = c_{0,X}\,(\nu/\nu_{\mathrm{ref},X})^{\gamma_X}$, which we place on
an absolute scale with each telescope's per-channel radiometer conversion
$\sigma_{S,X}(\nu) = \mathrm{SEFD}_X(\nu)\,/\,[\sqrt{n_{\mathrm{pol}}\,\Delta\nu\,\Delta t}\,G_X(\nu)]$,
taking the system-equivalent flux density and primary-beam gain $G_X$ from the
DSA-110 \citep{Law2024} and CHIME/FRB \citep{CHIMEFRB2018,Michilli2021} flux
scales. We integrate over each instrument's observing band---CHIME over
$0.400$--$0.800\,\mathrm{GHz}$ and DSA over $1.311$--$1.499\,\mathrm{GHz}$,
\begin{equation}
E_{\mathrm{iso}} = \frac{4\pi D_L^2(z)}{1+z}\left[
  \int_{\nu_1^{C}}^{\nu_2^{C}} s_C\,F_{\mathrm{CHIME}}(\nu)\,d\nu
  + \int_{\nu_1^{D}}^{\nu_2^{D}} s_D\,F_{\mathrm{DSA}}(\nu)\,d\nu
\right],
\label{eq:eiso}
\end{equation}
with $D_L(z)$ from the fiducial \textit{Planck}\,2018 cosmology
\citep{Planck2018}, the $(1+z)$ bandwidth k-correction applied, and
$1\,\mathrm{Jy\,ms\,Hz} = 10^{-29}\,\mathrm{J\,m^{-2}}$. This band-restricted
integral constrains the energy released within the observed spectral envelope:
it avoids the large, model-dependent extrapolation that a single power-law fit
across the full $0.4$--$1.5\,\mathrm{GHz}$ span would require, at the cost of not
counting flux in the unobserved $0.8$--$1.3\,\mathrm{GHz}$ gap.

Two properties of this definition must be stated prominently. First, the
observed bands map to \emph{per-burst} rest-frame intervals: CHIME's
$0.400$--$0.800\,\mathrm{GHz}$ samples the rest-frame band
$[0.400(1+z),\,0.800(1+z)]\,\mathrm{GHz}$ and DSA's
$1.311$--$1.499\,\mathrm{GHz}$ samples $[1.311(1+z),\,1.499(1+z)]\,\mathrm{GHz}$,
so the rest-frame window shifts from burst to burst. These band-restricted
energies are therefore \emph{not} comparable to literature energies computed
over a fixed rest-frame band, and cannot be placed on a standard FRB luminosity
function without a spectral-model extrapolation that we deliberately avoid here;
we report them as observed-band quantities and, where a comparison is needed,
provide a fixed rest-frame-band variant alongside. Second, the single $(1+z)$ in
Eq.~\ref{eq:eiso} is the bandwidth (time-dilation) k-correction, which is common
to both bands; the differing per-band spectral indices $\gamma_C,\gamma_D$ enter
only the in-band flux integrals $\int F_X(\nu)\,d\nu$, not the k-correction, so
the two-band sum is internally consistent despite the single $(1+z)$ factor.

\label{sec:conclusions}

We draw conclusions here for the association tests, the foreground census, and
the dispersion-budget decomposition; conclusions on the scattering fits, the
scintillation products, the turbulence parameters, and the burst energies follow
in the corresponding sections.

\begin{itemize}
\item \textit{Association.} All twelve candidate pairs are genuine
co-detections: each has a chance-coincidence probability $P_{\rm cc}<10^{-8}$
under conservative, chance-maximizing windows and timing residuals consistent with
the inter-site geometric delay under the shared DSA-DM reference convention.
Eight of the twelve additionally pass an independent CHIME--DSA DM-agreement
check; the other four rest on position and timing alone.

\item \textit{Foreground census.} The FRB~20230307A sightline is the only one in
the twelve-burst sample that passes within $R_{500}$ of a foreground cluster,
contributing an intracluster column of order $160\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$
(uncertain at the factor-of-two level, $\approx100$--$560\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$
across the profile choice and the cluster-parameter systematics).
The intervening circumgalactic and intracluster contribution to the dispersion
budget is therefore appreciable on that sightline alone and small on the rest;
the confirmed foreground galaxies lie in the outer circumgalactic medium, where
individual columns are minor. The census reaches only as deep as the wide-field
catalogs available at this declination, so a sightline without a confirmed
intervening system is unconstrained by survey depth, not demonstrably free of
foreground gas.

\item \textit{Dispersion budget.} With $\mathrm{DM_{host}}$ forward-modeled as a
posterior rather than an arithmetic residual, the decomposition leaves host
contributions that are a minority of the extragalactic column on every
redshift-constrained sightline (median $\mathrm{DM_{host}}$ from
$\approx10$ to $210\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$, with broad cosmology-dominated
uncertainties): the extragalactic column is dominated by the cosmological
term, not the host. The intervening term is concentrated on the single
cluster-crossing sightline, and the two highest-redshift sightlines are
consistent with zero host ($P(\mathrm{DM_{host}}<0)\approx0.45$), their
formerly negative point residuals reflecting the skew of the cosmological
distribution rather than an unphysical host. Sightlines without spectroscopic
host redshifts are excluded from all distance-dependent statements.
\end{itemize}

## 1. Draft-scaffolding language that will render in the PDF

These are the clearest outliers — internal workflow vocabulary sitting in text that compiles into the paper, not in `%` comments.

- **Literal placeholder brackets in the abstract.** `main.tex:46–60` contain five un-commented `[SLOT: …]` markers (joint scattering results, dispersion-budget decomposition, scintillation constraints, burst energies, physical conclusion). These are not comments — they typeset into the abstract. No published abstract carries bracketed to-do slots.
- **"citable / restored as citable measurements"** — `observations.tex:41` ("quantities are citable") and `results.tex:153` ("restored as citable measurements"). "Citable" is internal trust-ladder vocabulary from `CONTEXT.md`, not something a reader should see; the field says "reported" or "measured."
- **"deferred pending completion of the scattering analysis"** appears verbatim in `observations.tex:43`, `budget.tex:189`, `results.tex:71`, `appendix.tex:145`, `methods.tex:56`. Announcing inside the paper that a result is withheld pending your own future work is a lab-notebook register, not a manuscript one.
- **"current" as a draft-state flag** — `results.tex:152–154` ("The component-multiplicity audit is already informative… before the turbulence parameters are restored"; "the current FRB 20220310F… panel"; "eleven current joint-model… panels") and `budget.tex:255` ("current rows with missed sub-components remain diagnostic"). "Current," "already informative," and "remain diagnostic only until…" describe the state of your pipeline, not the physics.
- **"a trusted per-band amplitude fit"** — `methods.tex:14`. "Trusted" is trust-reset vocabulary; a methods section says "a well-constrained" or "a validated" fit.

Separately, the section bodies are largely `% TODO(...)` comment blocks (`discussion.tex` is almost entirely TODOs; `results.tex`, `conclusions.tex`, `budget.tex`, `observations.tex` each carry them). Those don't render, but they mean Discussion/Conclusions are essentially unwritten — worth knowing if you were treating the draft as complete.

## 2. Terminology inconsistencies (same thing named three ways)

- **The photo-z classifier** is called **"WISE–PanSTARRS–STRM"** (`observations.tex:108`), **"PanSTARRS-STRM … 'unsure' class"** (`observations.tex:139`), and **"PS1-STRM"** throughout `foreground_table.tex`. The published name (Beck et al. 2021) is **PS1-STRM**; pick one and use it everywhere.
- **Survey data release mismatch.** Prose cites **"DESI Legacy … DR9"** (`observations.tex:126,160,163`) and the abstract text says the discovery stage uses "DESI Legacy Imaging Surveys," but `budget_table.tex:57` (note u) says the footprint is **"DESI Legacy DR8-North."** DR8 vs DR9 should be reconciled.
- **Contradictory description of the Zhou 2021 estimator.** `observations.tex:106` calls it the **"deep-learning estimator of [Zhou2021]"**; `observations.tex:127` calls the same catalog the **"[Zhou2021] random-forest estimator."** These are mutually exclusive characterizations of one method (it is random-forest). One is wrong.
- **Cluster-catalog provenance.** `observations.tex` describes querying **PSZ2, MCXC, MCXC-II** X-ray/SZ catalogs and then says "at the sample's declination every matched cluster comes from the optical catalog," while the budget/foreground tables and `CONTEXT.md` state all clusters are from **Wen & Han 2024** only. The X-ray/SZ machinery is described but never actually sources a system — reads as leftover text.

## 3. Coined or non-standard terms used as if standard

- **"chance-maximizing windows"** — `toa.tex:72`, `results.tex:15`, `conclusions.tex:12`. A coined term. It is defined in place, but the field usage is "conservative" or "worst-case" windows; "chance-maximizing" is your own coinage repeated as if established.
- **"co-model / co-models" (verb)** — abstract `main.tex:41`, `observations.tex:57`, `budget.tex:122`. Understandable but non-standard; "jointly model" is the field phrasing.
- **"sub-floor offset"** — `toa.tex:159` ("a coherent, sub-floor offset"). Informal; means "below the quoted uncertainty floor" — say that.
- **"soft disk"** — `observations.tex:183` (sightline_halo_grid caption), describing a marker's rendering ("each halo is a soft disk"). This is plotting-implementation language in a physics caption.

{"filename":"observations.tex","content_type":"text/x-latex","size_bytes":13245,"content":"\section{Observations and Sample}
\label{sec:obs}

The working sample is the set of candidate fast-radio-burst co-detections seen
by CHIME/FRB \citep{CHIMEFRB2018} at $0.4$--$0.8$\,GHz and by DSA-110
\citep{Law2024} at $\sim$1.4\,GHz between 2022 February and 2024 February. The
defining asset of the sample is that each candidate event is observed across a
wide frequency baseline, which---as developed in Section~\ref{sec:jointfit}---can
separate scattering time from frequency scaling more strongly than a single-band
fit. The twelve bursts, their TNS designations, sky positions,
detection epochs, and association diagnostics are listed in
Table~\ref{tab:sample}. The timing residuals in that table use the shared
DSA-DM reference convention; the per-telescope DM values and their CHIME--DSA
agreement are provided as a machine-readable provenance table in the archival
data release (Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}).

\input{sample_table.tex}

\subsection{Dynamic spectra and reduction}
\label{sec:data}

For each co-detection we analyze a pair of total-intensity dynamic spectra---one
per facility---derived from the CHIME/FRB and DSA-110 baseband recordings
(24 spectra in total; per-spectrum provenance and array geometry accompany the
archival data release, Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}). Each spectrum is
loaded with its frequency axis
standardized to ascending order and its per-channel bandpass normalized, so the
fitted amplitudes are free of the band-edge rolloff. All fits, quality gates,
and figures in this paper are produced with a common open-source reduction and
fitting pipeline, which we reference throughout for reproducibility. We do not catalog the fine
baseband morphology of each burst here; the only morphological distinction the
analysis depends on---whether a burst resolves into more than one temporal
component---is treated where it matters, in the multi-component fit of
Section~\ref{sec:multicomp}.

\subsection{Scattering fits}
\label{sec:obs-scatt}

The Methods section defines the joint two-band model, the $\beta$-based
scattering closure, the temporal multiplicity treatment, and the quality gates
used to decide which per-sightline quantities are citable
(Sections~\ref{sec:jointfit} and~\ref{sec:multicomp}). Scattering-fit results
are deferred pending completion of the scattering analysis
(Section~\ref{sec:results-alpha}).

% TODO(obs-scattering): Reinsert any joint-fit montage only after the fit
% products shown in the figure have passed the re-validation contract.

\subsection{Milky Way foreground contributions}
\label{sec:obs-mw}

To characterize the expected propagation effects contributed by the Milky Way, we query the NE2025 Galactic electron density model \citep{Ocker2026} along each of the twelve co-detection sightlines. The expected Galactic dispersion measure ($\text{DM}_{\text{MW}}$) is integrated to a distance of 30\,kpc. The pulse-broadening timescale ($\tau_{\text{MW}}$) and scintillation bandwidth ($\Delta\nu_{\text{MW}}$) are scaled from the NE2025 output at 1\,GHz to the center of the CHIME/FRB band (600\,MHz) and the DSA-110 band (1.4\,GHz) assuming Kolmogorov scaling ($\tau \propto \nu^{-4.4}$ and $\Delta\nu_d \propto \nu^{4.4}$).

We flag one methodological asymmetry deliberately. This Galactic prediction
uses a \emph{fixed} Kolmogorov frequency index ($\alpha=4.4$) to scale
$\tau_{\text{MW}}$ between bands, whereas the extragalactic scattering analysis
of Section~\ref{sec:jointfit} co-models the frequency scaling with the pulse
shape through the turbulence index $\beta$ rather than fixing $\alpha$. We keep
the fixed-$\alpha$ Kolmogorov value here as a fiducial for the \emph{Galactic}
term because NE2025 is itself calibrated on a Kolmogorov medium and because the
predicted Galactic broadening is sub-microsecond on every high-latitude
sightline (below), so the choice of Galactic scaling index cannot affect the
extragalactic attribution there. On the low-latitude sightlines, where
$\tau_{\text{MW}}$ is non-negligible, we carry this as an explicit systematic
rather than propagate the fitted $\beta$ posterior through a model (NE2025) that
does not itself expose one.

As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:ne2025_mw}, the sightlines exhibit strongly
latitude-dependent behavior. High-latitude sightlines ($|b| > 30^\circ$)
present minimal Galactic contamination, with expected Milky Way dispersion
contributions $\text{DM}_{\text{MW}} \lesssim 41$\,pc\,cm$^{-3}$ and
sub-microsecond scattering broadening at CHIME frequencies
($\tau_{\text{MW}} \lesssim 0.1\,\mu$s). Low-latitude sightlines
($|b| < 15^\circ$) carry substantial Galactic contributions, with
$\text{DM}_{\text{MW}}$ reaching $\approx 95$\,pc\,cm$^{-3}$ and predicted
CHIME-band scattering timescales of $\tau_{\text{MW}} \gtrsim 30\,\mu$s,
which can dominate or mask the intrinsic extragalactic scattering.

The $\text{DM}_{\text{MW}}$ quoted in this section is the NE2025 \emph{disk}
(ISM) column alone. In the dispersion budget of
Section~\ref{sec:dm} (Table~\ref{tab:budget}) we add the
$40\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$ Milky-Way halo prior to this disk term, so the
$\mathrm{DM_{MW}}$ column there ($74$--$137\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$) is larger
than the $\lesssim95\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$ disk-only values here by that fixed
halo offset; the two are consistent once the halo term is accounted for.

\begin{figure*}
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/ne2025_mw_characterization_nside32.pdf}
    \caption{NE2025 Galactic electron density model predictions. Top row: All-sky Mollweide maps (in Galactic coordinates) showing predicted dispersion measure ($\text{DM}_{\text{MW}}$, left), pulse-broadening timescale ($\tau_{\text{MW}}$ at 1\,GHz, middle), and scintillation bandwidth ($\Delta\nu_{\text{MW}}$ at 1\,GHz, right). Yellow stars indicate the positions of the twelve sightlines. Bottom row: Individual sightline values plotted against absolute Galactic latitude $|b|$ for dispersion (left), scaled scattering timescale (middle; CHIME at 600\,MHz in orange, DSA-110 at 1.4\,GHz in blue), and scaled scintillation bandwidth (right). Scatter plots show values calculated assuming Kolmogorov scaling ($\alpha = 4.4$) \citep{Ocker2026}.}
    \label{fig:ne2025_mw}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Foreground-galaxy search}
\label{sec:obs-fg}

Before the intervening dispersion and scattering terms can be computed, each
sightline must be searched for foreground galaxies and clusters, and every
candidate assigned a redshift and a foreground/background verdict. We run the
search in two stages---discovery followed by external-catalog
validation---and record the result in Table~\ref{tab:foreground}.

The discovery stage searches a wide cone around each localized burst. At the
sample's high declination ($+70^\circ$ to $+74^\circ$) only all-sky or northern
catalogs provide coverage. For galaxy-scale halos we query the DESI Legacy
Imaging Surveys photometric-redshift catalog \citep[the deep-learning estimator
of][]{Zhou2021}, GLADE+, and SDSS~DR12, supplemented by the
WISE--PanSTARRS--STRM photometric classifier \citep{Beck2021} for sources
without a Legacy match; for clusters we query the DESI Legacy/WISE cluster
catalog of \citet{WenHan2024}---which supplies $M_{500}$, $R_{500}$, and,
through its DESI members, a spectroscopic redshift---together with the all-sky
Planck Sunyaev--Zel'dovich (PSZ2) and X-ray (MCXC, MCXC-II) catalogs; at the
sample's declination every matched cluster comes from the optical catalog.
The search apertures set the census completeness and are fixed in physical
units. Galaxy cones extend to a fixed proper impact parameter at the
circumgalactic scale, and the recovered foreground galaxies span
$b\approx60$--$280\,\mathrm{kpc}$; cluster cones are far wider
($\sim\!5\,\mathrm{Mpc}$), and a cluster is retained when the sightline passes
within twice its virial radius ($\lesssim2\,R_{200}\approx3\,R_{500}$), so a
sightline that grazes a cluster outskirt is still recovered. Only crossings
within $R_{500}$ enter the dispersion budget (Section~\ref{sec:foreground}); the
wider retention aperture exists so the excluded near-misses can be shown
explicitly (Appendix~\ref{app:clusters}).

Validation cross-matches every candidate, by coordinate, against three
independent redshift sources: the DESI Legacy Survey DR9 photometric redshifts
(the \citealt{Zhou2021} random-forest estimator, which supplies a per-object
redshift error and a morphological type), DESI~DR1 spectroscopic redshifts, and
the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED). Matches use a $5''$ radius for
galaxies and a $90''$ radius for cluster centroids. The adopted redshift follows
a fixed priority---DESI spectroscopic, then Legacy spectroscopic, then Legacy
photometric (carrying its $1\sigma$ error), then NED---and a DESI spectrum
classified as a star is rejected outright, so a foreground stellar velocity can
never be adopted as a system redshift. A candidate is \emph{confirmed} when its
redshift (or photometric redshift plus $1\sigma$) lies below the host,
\emph{refuted} when it lies above, and \emph{inconclusive} when it falls within
$1\sigma$ of the host, when the host redshift is a placeholder, or when no
trustworthy redshift or galaxy classification exists (including the
PanSTARRS-STRM ``unsure'' class).

The census evaluated 35 candidate systems along the twelve sightlines.
Table~\ref{tab:foreground} lists the 28 that remain relevant to the budget:
15 confirmed foreground (14 galaxy halos and the single cluster whose
sightline passes within $R_{500}$) and 13 inconclusive---candidates the
available redshifts could not place on either side of the host, which
therefore bound the systematic uncertainty on each sightline's intervening
column. The remaining 7 candidates were refuted as background by the
validation stage and are omitted from the table, as are candidates rejected
outright as stars. A further 14 spectroscopically confirmed
foreground clusters lie along these sightlines at impact parameters beyond
$R_{500}$; dynamically irrelevant to the dispersion budget, they are likewise
omitted---the \citet{WenHan2024} catalog reaches group scale
($M_{500}>0.47\times10^{14}\,M_\odot$), so several are low-mass systems. Because
only wide-field catalogs reach this declination, a sightline with no confirmed
system is unconstrained by survey depth rather than demonstrably foreground-free;
the budget of Section~\ref{sec:foreground} treats such sightlines accordingly.

Of the 15 confirmed foreground systems, nine carry a DESI or NED spectroscopic
(or the single cluster's DESI-member) redshift; the remaining six rest on a
DESI Legacy DR9 photometric redshift alone. Our confirmation criterion
($z_{\rm phot}+1\sigma < z_{\rm host}$) admits, per object, the $\sim16\%$
Gaussian probability that the true redshift exceeds $z_{\rm phot}+1\sigma$, plus
the few-percent catastrophic-outlier rate characteristic of Legacy DR9
photometric redshifts. Across the six photometric confirmations this amounts to
of order one expected misclassification of a background galaxy as foreground.
Each of these six lies in the outer circumgalactic medium
($b\approx105$--$280\,\mathrm{kpc}$) and contributes only a small dispersion
column, so a misclassification would remove at most that object's column from
$\mathrm{DM_{int}}$; we therefore carry the summed photometric-confirmation
column as a one-sided systematic on $\mathrm{DM_{int}}$ for the affected
sightlines (Section~\ref{sec:foreground}) rather than treating those
confirmations as exact.
Figure~\ref{fig:sightline_halo_grid} shows the foreground-halo population used
for this census as a function of redshift and signed impact parameter.

{"filename":"results.tex","content_type":"text/x-latex","size_bytes":9375,"content":"\section{Results}
\label{sec:results}

The association tests, the foreground census, and the dispersion-budget
decomposition are reported below. The scattering fits for individual sightlines,
the scintillation products, the turbulence parameters, the burst energies, and
the measured-versus-predicted scattering comparison are presented in the
following subsections.

\FloatBarrier
\subsection{Association of the co-detections}
\label{sec:results-association}

All twelve candidate pairs pass the association tests of
Section~\ref{sec:toa}. Under the conservative, chance-maximizing windows of
Section~\ref{sec:toa-pcc}, every pair has a chance-coincidence probability
$P_{\rm cc}<10^{-8}$---fewer than one in a hundred million such pairings would be
expected to arise by chance---and passes the positional-coincidence check; the
timing residuals, referenced to
400\,MHz under the shared DSA-DM convention, are consistent with the
predicted inter-site geometric delay (Table~\ref{tab:sample}). The eight
bursts with constrained CHIME-side dispersion measures also pass the
CHIME--DSA DM-agreement check; the remaining four are retained as
position-and-timing associations, since their CHIME sub-band fits do not
provide a constraining independent DM. We therefore treat all twelve pairs as
genuine co-detections in what follows.

\FloatBarrier
\subsection{Per-sightline dispersion budget}
\label{sec:results-budget}
\label{sec:budget}

Table~\ref{tab:budget} reports the decomposition of Eq.~\ref{eq:dmbudget}
along the twelve sightlines. Three features organize the sample. First, the
intervening term is strongly concentrated: summed over the confirmed
foreground systems of Section~\ref{sec:obs-fg}, $\mathrm{DM_{int}}$ exceeds
$100\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$ on only one sightline (FRB~20230307A,
$241\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$, dominated by the intracluster column of
Section~\ref{sec:dominant-systems}), while the remaining sightlines carry
$\lesssim 85\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$ and four tabulate
$\mathrm{DM_{int}}=0$---for two distinct reasons. FRB~20220506D and
FRB~20240122A lie outside the deep-imaging footprints, so their sightlines
are constrained only by the shallow all-sky layers and their intervening
terms are unconstrained rather than measured to be zero (note~u). FRB~20240203A
and FRB~20240229A carry confirmed foreground halos, but those halos lie
beyond the virial radii implied by their assumed masses, so the modeled
columns vanish; their zeros are conditional on the mass assumption (note~m),
and FRB~20240203A is additionally outside the deep footprints. One sightline
(FRB~20230814B) crosses a foreground halo in the
galaxy-interior regime ($b \approx 4.6$\,kpc), where the reported column is
capped at its $b=0.1\,R_{\rm vir}$ value rather than extrapolated through the
core. Second, the forward-modeled host posteriors
(Section~\ref{sec:dm}, Appendix~\ref{app:host-forward-model}) are broad and
asymmetric, dominated by the cosmological scatter: their medians span
$\mathrm{DM_{host}}\approx10$--$210\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$, the largest on
FRB~20240229A ($211^{+46}_{-112}\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$), and every sightline is
consistent with a host contributing a minority of the extragalactic column once
the width of the cosmological term is folded in. Third, the two
highest-redshift sightlines (FRB~20220310F, FRB~20221203A), whose
mean-subtracted point residuals were negative, have host posteriors consistent
with zero ($P(\mathrm{DM_{host}}<0)\approx0.45$; Table~\ref{tab:budget}); those
negative point values reflected the skew of $P(\mathrm{DM_{cosmic}}\,|\,z)$ at
these redshifts, not an unphysical host. The three sightlines without
spectroscopic host redshifts are excluded from all distance-dependent terms
(Table~\ref{tab:budget}, note p).

The predicted intervening pulse broadening from the thin-screen two-phase
model is small everywhere: $\tau_{\mathrm{int}} \le 0.024$\,ms at 1\,GHz,
with the largest values on the sightlines whose confirmed halos are crossed
at the smallest impact parameters. The comparison of these predictions with
the measured scattering times is deferred to the scattering analysis
(Section~\ref{sec:results-alpha}).

% TODO(results-budget-scattering): Restore the measured-vs-predicted
% scattering comparison (fig:budget overlay, tau_obs column) only after the
% V1 fit ladder clears.

\input{budget_table}

\subsubsection{Dominant intervening systems}
\label{sec:dominant-systems}

Of the twelve sightlines, only one crosses a foreground cluster within its
characteristic radius. The FRB~20230307A sightline passes at $b/R_{500}=0.83$
from the \citet{WenHan2024} cluster J115120.4$+$714435 ($z=0.200$,
$\log M_{500}=14.1$), for which the two-phase modified-NFW model predicts an
intracluster column of order $160\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$
(Figure~\ref{fig:clusters_icm}). This column is uncertain at the factor-of-two
level: an X-ray/SZ-motivated isothermal $\beta$-model with the same $M_{500}$
and a cosmic gas fraction gives a somewhat higher central value
($\approx250\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$), and propagating the richness-based
$M_{500}$ ($\sim\!0.2$\,dex scatter), the gas fraction ($f_{\rm gas}=0.10$--$0.16$),
and the profile shape spans $\approx100$--$560\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$
(Appendix~\ref{app:host-forward-model}). The mNFW profile is calibrated on
$\sim\!L_\star$ halos \citep{ProchaskaZheng2019} and is not guaranteed to hold at
$\log M_{500}=14.1$; its value sits near the low end of the $\beta$-model range
and should be read as a conservative estimate. This is nonetheless the single
dominant intervening system in the sample. The other foreground clusters recovered along this and the two
other cluster-bearing sightlines all lie beyond $R_{500}$, where the modeled
column drops steeply; they are excluded from the budget even where the profile
remains formally nonzero, and none approaches the FRB~20230307A cluster in
predicted contribution.

The confirmed foreground galaxies lie at impact parameters of tens to a few
hundred kpc, in the outer circumgalactic medium rather than the galaxy interior.
Each therefore adds only a small dispersion, and no single galaxy halo dominates
its sightline's intervening column.
The intervening dispersion in this sample is thus concentrated almost entirely on
the one cluster-crossing sightline and is small on the rest; the sightlines
carrying no confirmed within-aperture system are limited by survey depth
(Section~\ref{sec:obs-fg}), not shown to be free of foreground gas.

\FloatBarrier
\subsection{Scintillation and screen attribution}
\label{sec:results-scintillation}

{"filename":"budget.tex","content_type":"text/x-latex","size_bytes":15936,"content":"% Core methods section. The observed DM decomposes as
%   DM_obs = DM_MW,ISM + DM_MW,halo + <DM_cosmic>(z) + DM_intervening + DM_host,
% with DM_MW,ISM from NE2001/YMW16 (pygedm), DM_MW,halo a 40 pc/cm^3 prior
% (Yamasaki & Totani 2020), <DM_cosmic>(z) the Macquart relation
% (Macquart et al. 2020; f_IGM=0.84, chi_e=0.875), and DM_intervening a two-phase
% (hot mNFW + clumpy cool) CGM column for each intersecting foreground galaxy,
% reported raw and capped at b = 0.1 R_vir (the galaxy-interior regime).
%
% Scattering: measured tau_1GHz from nested-sampling fits (quality-gated on
% reduced chi^2, with R^2/normality informational only), compared against the
% predicted intervening tau from a thin-screen two-phase model.

\subsection{Dispersion Measure Decomposition}
\label{sec:dm}

The observed dispersion measure of each co-detection separates into Galactic,
cosmological, host, and (where the sightline intersects a foreground galaxy)
intervening contributions \citep{CordesChatterjee2019},
\begin{equation}
\mathrm{DM_{obs}} = \mathrm{DM_{MW}} + \langle\mathrm{DM_{cosmic}}\rangle(z)
+ \mathrm{DM_{int}} + \mathrm{DM_{host}},
\label{eq:dmbudget}
\end{equation}
with the Galactic term $\mathrm{DM_{MW}} = \mathrm{DM_{MW,ISM}} +
\mathrm{DM_{MW,halo}}$ split between disk and halo. We estimate the disk
contribution $\mathrm{DM_{MW,ISM}}$ from the NE2025 \citep{Ocker2026}
electron-density model integrated to $30\,\mathrm{kpc}$ (consistent with the
Galactic characterization of Section~\ref{sec:obs-fg}), with the older NE2001
\citep{CordesLazio2002} and YMW16 \citep{YMW2017} models retained as
cross-checks, and adopt a $\sim$40\,pc\,cm$^{-3}$ Milky-Way halo prior
\citep{YamasakiTotani2020}. The
cosmological mean $\langle\mathrm{DM_{cosmic}}\rangle(z)$ follows the Macquart
relation \citep{Macquart2020}. For the sightlines that cross a foreground
galaxy we model the intervening circumgalactic column $\mathrm{DM_{int}}$ as a
two-phase (hot modified-NFW plus clumpy cool) medium, taking the mNFW gas
profile from \citet{ProchaskaZheng2019}. Such intervening halos can imprint
measurable dispersion and, where the gas is turbulent, scattering on FRB
sightlines \citep{Ocker2021}, although an individual quiescent halo may instead
yield only an upper limit on its scattering contribution \citep{Prochaska2019}.

Rather than attribute the arithmetic residual of the mean-subtracted budget to
the host, we forward-model $\mathrm{DM_{host}}$ as a posterior. The cosmological
term is drawn from the full, skewed conditional distribution
$P(\mathrm{DM_{cosmic}}\,|\,z)$---the \citet{Macquart2020} functional form with
fractional scatter $\sigma_{\rm DM}=F\,z^{-1/2}$, $F\in[0.25,0.40]$
\citep{McQuinn2014, James2022}---and the Galactic-disk, Galactic-halo, and
intervening columns are drawn from their own priors
(Appendix~\ref{app:host-forward-model}). We evaluate
$\mathrm{DM_{host}}=\mathrm{DM_{obs}}-\mathrm{DM_{MW}}-\mathrm{DM_{cosmic}}-
\mathrm{DM_{int}}$ per draw. Because $P(\mathrm{DM_{cosmic}})$ is right-skewed,
subtracting its \emph{mean}---as an arithmetic residual does---biases the
inferred host low; sampling the full distribution removes that bias and returns
an asymmetric posterior with a well-defined $P(\mathrm{DM_{host}}<0)$ per
sightline (Table~\ref{tab:budget}, Appendix~\ref{app:host-forward-model}).

\subsection{Intervening Foreground Galaxies and Clusters}
\label{sec:foreground}

The intervening term $\mathrm{DM_{int}}$ is sourced only where a sightline
actually crosses foreground circumgalactic or intracluster gas. We therefore
build $\mathrm{DM_{int}}$ from the confirmed foreground systems of the census
(Section~\ref{sec:obs-fg}, Table~\ref{tab:foreground}), and only from those
whose impact parameter places the sightline inside the gas: for galaxies we
integrate the column to the halo and cap the reported value at $b = 0.1\,R_{\rm
vir}$, the galaxy-interior regime; for clusters we require the sightline to pass
within $R_{500}$ ($b/R_{500}\le 1$), a conservative aperture that across the
whole sample selects a single cluster (FRB~20230307A, J115120.4$+$714435 at
$b/R_{500}=0.83$). Refuted and inconclusive systems, and confirmed systems whose
sightline passes outside this aperture, do not contribute.

For each retained system we model the gas as a two-phase medium---a hot
modified-NFW component \citep{ProchaskaZheng2019} plus a clumpy cool
component---and integrate the electron column along the impact-parameter chord.
The cluster contribution uses the $M_{500}$ and $R_{500}$ of the
\citet{WenHan2024} catalog to fix the halo, evaluating the intracluster
dispersion on the modified-NFW profile at the sightline's impact parameter.
The $b\le R_{500}$ cut retains just the one cluster that pierces the halo; the
per-cluster dispersion columns for all four foreground clusters along the
FRB~20230307A sightline are shown in Figure~\ref{fig:clusters_icm}
(Appendix~\ref{app:clusters}).

\subsection{Scattering Attribution}
\label{sec:scattering}

We measure the pulse-broadening time and its frequency scaling from the
co-detected dynamic spectra and compare them against the scattering predicted
by each candidate screen: the Milky-Way ISM, an intervening circumgalactic
medium, or a host galaxy, where the host can dominate the scattering of a
heavily broadened burst \citep{Ocker2022}. The fits are produced with a common
open-source reduction and fitting pipeline.

\subsubsection{The joint two-band fit}
\label{sec:jointfit}
\label{sec:beta-scattering-methods}

A single-band scattering fit cannot separate the scattering timescale from its
frequency scaling, because the pulse broadening
$\tau(\nu)=\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}\,(\nu/1\,\mathrm{GHz})^{-\alpha}$ enters a
single band only through its value at that band's frequency: one band fixes the
product, not the slope. Co-detection breaks this degeneracy---the $\sim$1\,GHz
lever arm between CHIME/FRB ($\sim$0.4--0.8\,GHz) and DSA-110
($\sim$1.4\,GHz) measures the frequency scaling directly---but breaking the
degeneracy is not the same as measuring $\alpha$ in a physically consistent way.

The pulse-broadening function (PBF) shape and the scattering index $\alpha$ are
not independent observables. Both descend from the same turbulence spectrum: a
power-law electron-density fluctuation spectrum
$P_n(q)\propto q^{-\beta}$ produces a phase structure function
$D_\phi(\rho)\propto\rho^{\beta-2}$, whose Fourier transform determines the
angular brightness distribution and hence the PBF shape, while the diffractive
scale and Fresnel arguments give the frequency scaling
$\tau\propto\nu^{-2\beta/(\beta-2)}$ for a thin screen---i.e.\
$\alpha=2\beta/(\beta-2)$ \citep{Rickett1990, Cordes2025}. The common
one-sided exponential PBF,
$p_E(t,\tau)=\tau^{-1}e^{-t/\tau}\,\Theta(t)$, corresponds to a square-law
structure function $D_\phi(\rho)\propto\rho^2$ ($\beta=4$), for which
$\alpha=4$. A Kolmogorov spectrum ($\beta=11/3$) instead yields a
power-law PBF tail $p_d(t)\propto t^{-\beta/2}$ and $\alpha\approx4.4$
\citep{Cordes2025}.

Because $\beta$ determines both the PBF shape and $\alpha$, they must be
co-modeled. Choosing a fixed PBF family and freely varying $\alpha$ inside it
is physically inconsistent: the exponential PBF assumes square-law
statistics, which already imply $\alpha\approx4$. If the true medium is
Kolmogorov---or anything other than square-law---fitting an exponential PBF
will absorb the shape mismatch into a biased $\alpha$ \citep{Cordes2025}.

We therefore replace the phenomenological pair $(\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}},\alpha)$
with the turbulence spectral index $\beta$ as the fundamental parameter. For
each $\beta$ in the fit, the PBF shape is computed directly from the
corresponding phase structure function $D_\phi(\rho)\propto\rho^{\beta-2}$, and
the frequency scaling index follows as $\alpha=2\beta/(\beta-2)$ under the
thin-screen assumption. The free parameters of the joint fit are
$(\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}},\beta)$ and the per-telescope pulse parameters
(arrival time $t_0$, intrinsic width $\zeta$, and a residual dispersion
$\delta\mathrm{DM}$). The fit operates on the CHIME/FRB and DSA-110 dynamic
spectra simultaneously, with the PBF shape and $\alpha$ both derived from
$\beta$ at every likelihood evaluation rather than chosen independently.

Under this model, the Kolmogorov value $\beta=11/3$ ($\alpha\approx4.4$) and the
square-law limit $\beta=4$ ($\alpha=4$, exponential PBF) are specific points in
the physically allowed thin-screen inertial-range mapping. Because
$\alpha=2\beta/(\beta-2)$ decreases as $\beta$ increases in this regime, a
formally shallow empirical scaling is not interpreted by itself as a turbulence
spectral index; it instead indicates that the assumed scattering family, burst
morphology, or inertial-range closure is incomplete.

The square-law endpoint requires a further caution that we build into the
results rather than append as a caveat. When the diffractive scale drops below
the turbulence inner scale, the phase structure function is locally quadratic
and $\alpha\to4$ regardless of the underlying spectral index, so an
inner-scale-dominated medium and a genuine $\beta=4$ spectrum are observationally
degenerate \citep{Cordes2016, Cordes2025}. A posterior that concentrates at
$\beta=4$ is therefore \emph{not} reported as a measurement of square-law
turbulence: it marks the closure regime in which the thin-screen inertial-range
mapping no longer determines $\beta$. Accordingly, the per-sightline results
table (Section~\ref{sec:results-alpha}) will carry an explicit closure-regime
label---interior (a resolved $\beta$ within the inertial range) versus
square-law/inner-scale limited---alongside each entry, so that no square-law
endpoint is read as an inertial-range spectral index.

\subsubsection{Sub-band validation of the frequency scaling}
\label{sec:subband}
\label{sec:beta-subband}

The frequency scaling recovered by the joint fit can be validated independently
of the PBF parametrization by fitting the scattering time directly in frequency
sub-bands. We split each band into sub-bands (four across CHIME, three across
DSA-110), collapse each to a one-dimensional profile, and fit a scattered
pulse---a Gaussian intrinsic shape convolved with a one-sided exponential tail,
the exponentially-modified Gaussian---to each sub-band independently, leaving the
scattering time $\tau$ free at every frequency. Because each $\tau_i$ is measured
per sub-band with no power law imposed, the slope of $\tau_i(\nu_i)$ is an
estimate of the frequency scaling that shares neither parametrization nor
likelihood with the joint two-dimensional fit.

This cross-check is a slope diagnostic, not an independent turbulence
measurement, and we treat it as such for a specific reason. Each sub-band $\tau_i$
is itself fit with an exponential (EMG) kernel---the $\beta=4$ family the joint
fit is designed to avoid privileging---so each $\tau_i$ inherits a shape-mismatch
bias wherever the true PBF is not exponential. The sub-band \emph{slope} is
informative only insofar as that bias is common to all sub-bands and cancels in
the ratio; it does not cancel when the mismatch is frequency-dependent, which is
the generic case (the fraction of the pulse in the power-law tail, and hence the
bias on a per-band exponential $\tau_i$, itself varies with frequency). We
therefore report the sub-band slope as a validation diagnostic---a consistency
check on the joint-fit frequency scaling---and never as a measurement of the
turbulence spectral index. The sub-band validation figure and any event-level
sub-band comparisons are deferred pending completion of the scattering
analysis.

% TODO(methods-subband-validation): Restore a sub-band validation artifact only
% after fit re-validation. The restored figure should state the included bursts,
% exclusion reasons, fit family, and whether the sub-band slopes are used as a
% validation diagnostic or as physical turbulence constraints.

\subsubsection{Gain-marginalized likelihood}
\label{sec:gainmarg}
\label{sec:beta-gainmarg}

The per-channel amplitude of an FRB spectrum is unknown and strongly modulated
by scintillation; carried as a free spectral envelope it leaves correlated
structure in the fit residuals and invalidates a $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test.
We therefore marginalize the per-channel gain analytically. With a Gaussian
prior on the gain the per-channel evidence is available in closed form (a
matched-filter likelihood), and the recovered gain absorbs both the burst
spectrum and the scintillation pattern. This removes the spectral and
scintillation structure from the residual, so that the reduced $\chi^2$ and the
lag-1 residual autocorrelation become meaningful quality gates, and it lowers
the sampled dimensionality.

Because the marginalization deliberately absorbs the scintillation pattern into
the gain, the scattering fit does not measure scintillation: the
decorrelation-bandwidth products used later
(Section~\ref{sec:results-scintillation}) come from a separate
autocorrelation-function analysis path applied to the same dynamic spectra, not
from this likelihood. The one interaction to guard against is at low
signal-to-noise, where a fully free per-channel gain can absorb genuine
scattering-induced spectral smearing into the spectral envelope and thereby bias
the scattering time low; we therefore keep the gain prior informative (finite
width) rather than flat, and verify on the final fits that the
$\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}$ posterior is stable against the gain-prior width before
quoting any low-$\tau$ result. We
sample the posterior by nested sampling \citep{Skilling2004}, as implemented in
\texttt{dynesty} \citep{Speagle2020}, and quality-gate each fit on its reduced
$\chi^2$ and residual whiteness, treating $R^2$ and residual normality as
informational.

\subsubsection{Multiple temporal components}
\label{sec:multicomp}
\label{sec:beta-multicomp}

Some bursts comprise more than one temporal sub-component, a common feature of
the resolved FRB population \citep{Hessels2019, Pleunis2021}. An unmodeled
second component is partially absorbed by a single scattered pulse---as a steeper
frequency scaling and a larger scattering time---and so biases the inferred
$\beta$, distorting both the fitted PBF shape and $\alpha$ simultaneously. We
extend the likelihood to $N$ temporal components per band, each contributing its
own $(t_0,\zeta)$ while $(\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}},\beta)$ remain shared across
both bands and all components, and we select the number of components per band
by Bayesian evidence subject to a residual check. Because the evidence
normalization differs between
likelihood variants, the component counts are compared on a single matched grid
in which even the one-component model is evaluated through the multi-component
likelihood, so that evidence differences are meaningful. Component arrival times
within a band are ordered and separated by a data-derived minimum spacing,
keeping the fit identifiable at every prior sample.

The evidence comparison is not by itself the final acceptance gate. For each
candidate multiplicity we inspect the two-dimensional data, best-fit model, and
whitened residual dynamic spectra in both bands. A component choice is rejected
and re-fit if coherent burst power remains in the residuals, or if the collapsed
profiles show a resolved sub-component that is absent from the model, even when
the scalar goodness-of-fit statistics are otherwise acceptable. This visual and
residual audit is part of the component-selection procedure: current rows with
missed sub-components remain diagnostic only until their higher-multiplicity
fits pass the same check. A clean single-component co-detection in our sample,
for which the joint fit returns a well-constrained $\beta$ with white residuals,
validates the method; accepted multi-component cases will be presented in
Section~\ref{sec:results-alpha}.
"}

{"filename":"methods.tex","content_type":"text/x-latex","size_bytes":3294,"content":"\section{Methods}
\label{sec:methods}

This section defines how the co-detections are associated, how the dispersion
and scattering budgets are modeled, and how the fitted quantities used in
Section~\ref{sec:results} are validated.

\input{sections/toa.tex}
\input{sections/budget.tex}

\subsection{Band-restricted burst energies}
\label{sec:methods-energies}

For each sightline with a spectroscopic host redshift and a trusted per-band
amplitude fit, we estimate the isotropic-equivalent burst energy directly
from the joint CHIME--DSA fit, without extrapolating either band's spectrum
beyond where it is constrained.
The fit returns a per-band spectral amplitude and index,
$F_X(\nu) = c_{0,X}\,(\nu/\nu_{\mathrm{ref},X})^{\gamma_X}$, which we place on
an absolute scale with each telescope's per-channel radiometer conversion
$\sigma_{S,X}(\nu) = \mathrm{SEFD}_X(\nu)\,/\,[\sqrt{n_{\mathrm{pol}}\,\Delta\nu\,\Delta t}\,G_X(\nu)]$,
taking the system-equivalent flux density and primary-beam gain $G_X$ from the
DSA-110 \citep{Law2024} and CHIME/FRB \citep{CHIMEFRB2018,Michilli2021} flux
scales. We integrate over each instrument's observing band---CHIME over
$0.400$--$0.800\,\mathrm{GHz}$ and DSA over $1.311$--$1.499\,\mathrm{GHz}$,
\begin{equation}
E_{\mathrm{iso}} = \frac{4\pi D_L^2(z)}{1+z}\left[
  \int_{\nu_1^{C}}^{\nu_2^{C}} s_C\,F_{\mathrm{CHIME}}(\nu)\,d\nu
  + \int_{\nu_1^{D}}^{\nu_2^{D}} s_D\,F_{\mathrm{DSA}}(\nu)\,d\nu
\right],
\label{eq:eiso}
\end{equation}
with $D_L(z)$ from the fiducial \textit{Planck}\,2018 cosmology
\citep{Planck2018}, the $(1+z)$ bandwidth k-correction applied, and
$1\,\mathrm{Jy\,ms\,Hz} = 10^{-29}\,\mathrm{J\,m^{-2}}$. This band-restricted
integral constrains the energy released within the observed spectral envelope:
it avoids the large, model-dependent extrapolation that a single power-law fit
across the full $0.4$--$1.5\,\mathrm{GHz}$ span would require, at the cost of not
counting flux in the unobserved $0.8$--$1.3\,\mathrm{GHz}$ gap.

Two properties of this definition must be stated prominently. First, the
observed bands map to \emph{per-burst} rest-frame intervals: CHIME's
$0.400$--$0.800\,\mathrm{GHz}$ samples the rest-frame band
$[0.400(1+z),\,0.800(1+z)]\,\mathrm{GHz}$ and DSA's
$1.311$--$1.499\,\mathrm{GHz}$ samples $[1.311(1+z),\,1.499(1+z)]\,\mathrm{GHz}$,
so the rest-frame window shifts from burst to burst. These band-restricted
energies are therefore \emph{not} comparable to literature energies computed
over a fixed rest-frame band, and cannot be placed on a standard FRB luminosity
function without a spectral-model extrapolation that we deliberately avoid here;
we report them as observed-band quantities and, where a comparison is needed,
provide a fixed rest-frame-band variant alongside. Second, the single $(1+z)$ in
Eq.~\ref{eq:eiso} is the bandwidth (time-dilation) k-correction, which is common
to both bands; the differing per-band spectral indices $\gamma_C,\gamma_D$ enter
only the in-band flux integrals $\int F_X(\nu)\,d\nu$, not the k-correction, so
the two-band sum is internally consistent despite the single $(1+z)$ factor.
Energy rows are deferred until the spectral
amplitudes, calibration path, host-redshift provenance, and inclusion rule are
ready to be described and cited in the manuscript.
"}
